channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Qunova Joins JHPC-Quantum Test User Program in Japan, Providing Algorithms For Advanced Industrial Computation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant step in Japan's national quantum strategy, as Qunova's optimization algorithms will now be tested on Japan's ambitious high-performance computing infrastructure through the JHPC-quantum initiative. The collaboration highlights how countries are increasingly prioritizing quantum software development alongside hardware investments, recognizing that practical quantum applications require sophisticated algorithmic frameworks. Qunova's focus on industrial computation problems could accelerate real-world quantum adoption in manufacturing and logistics sectors, areas where Japan has deep expertise and economic interests. This type of government-industry partnership demonstrates the maturing quantum ecosystem, where specialized software companies are becoming integral to national quantum competitiveness strategies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Qunova Computing, a pioneering maker of software for quantum computing, has signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with JHPC-quantum (Japan High-Performance Computing), a landmark Japanese national computing initiative funded by NEDO (New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization) under Japan’s Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry. The agreement designates Qunova […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Report: Quantum Computing Could Unlock More Than £100 Million in Value
item.description:
Curator's Take: This D-Wave commissioned survey reveals surprisingly bullish short-term expectations among UK enterprises, with nearly half anticipating nine-figure quantum value creation within just one year. While such aggressive timelines may reflect more optimism than current quantum capabilities can deliver, the findings underscore how seriously large organizations are now taking quantum computing as a strategic priority rather than a distant research curiosity. The survey's focus on immediate value creation suggests businesses are primarily looking at quantum annealing applications for optimization problems, where D-Wave's systems have demonstrated real-world advantages today, rather than waiting for fault-tolerant quantum computers that remain years away. This shift from "quantum someday" to "quantum now" thinking, even if overly ambitious, signals a maturing market where quantum solutions are increasingly viewed as practical business tools.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Forty-one percent of large UK enterprises surveyed estimate quantum computing could unlock more than £100 million in value to their business in as little as one year, according to new findings from Censuswide and D-Wave Quantum Inc. (“D-Wave” or the “Company”). That expectation is also reflected in action: 65% of the UK […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Installs Real-Time Control Component for Universal Photonic Quantum Computer
item.description:
Curator's Take: This installation of QuiX Quantum's Feed-Forward Control Unit represents a crucial step toward making photonic quantum computers practically viable, as real-time measurement feedback is essential for error correction and complex quantum algorithms in photonic systems. Unlike trapped ion or superconducting approaches, photonic quantum computers face unique challenges in maintaining quantum information flow, making this type of ultra-fast control hardware a critical bottleneck that the field has been working to solve. The FFCU addresses one of the fundamental engineering hurdles that has limited photonic quantum computing's scalability, potentially bringing this promising approach closer to competing with other quantum computing platforms. While still early-stage, this development signals growing maturity in photonic quantum hardware and could accelerate progress toward fault-tolerant photonic quantum systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuiX Quantum today announced the first installation of its Feed-Forward Control Unit (FFCU), a high-performance hardware component developed for the company’s universal photonic quantum computing architecture. The FFCU is designed to help the system respond to quantum measurements in real time, an essential requirement for photonic quantum computers that encode and […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quobly Secures €115 Million Series A to Bring Silicon-Based Quantum Computers to Market
item.description:
Curator's Take: This €115 million Series A for Quobly represents one of the largest quantum computing funding rounds in Europe and signals growing investor confidence in silicon-based quantum approaches as a viable alternative to superconducting and trapped-ion systems. The French company's focus on silicon quantum dots is particularly noteworthy because this technology could potentially leverage existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure, offering a path to more scalable and cost-effective quantum computers. Quobly's ambitious 2026 timeline for commercial deployment puts them in direct competition with established players like IBM and Google, while their silicon approach could provide advantages in terms of operating temperature and manufacturing compatibility. The substantial funding reflects the quantum industry's maturation, as investors increasingly back companies with clear commercialization roadmaps rather than pure research plays.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quobly, a French quantum computing company, today announced the closing of a €115 million Series A financing to accelerate the industrialization of its silicon-based quantum computers and bring its first commercial product to market by the end of 2026. From technology validation to commercial deployment at scale This Series A […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Microsoft Reports Advances in Majorana 2 Following Debate Over Last Year’s Topological Claims
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement represents a crucial moment for Microsoft's controversial topological qubit program, which faced significant scrutiny last year when researchers questioned claims about achieving Majorana particles. The reported 1,000-fold improvement in qubit stability, if validated, could vindicate Microsoft's unique approach and potentially leapfrog current noisy quantum systems that struggle with error rates. Topological qubits promise inherent error protection through their mathematical properties, making this development particularly significant for the field's ultimate goal of fault-tolerant quantum computing. While Microsoft's 2029 timeline for scalable quantum computers remains ambitious, these stability gains suggest their unconventional bet on topological protection may finally be paying dividends.

Insider Brief Microsoft is reporting that it has achieved a more than 1,000-fold improvement in the stability of its topological qubits, a result the company argues brings its long-debated quantum computing approach significantly closer to practical machines and supports a roadmap targeting a scalable quantum computer by 2029. The announcement, detailed in a Microsoft technical […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IQM and Real Asset Acquisition Corp. Announce Upsized $146 Million PIPE with New Commitment from Ilmarinen
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals growing institutional confidence in quantum hardware companies as pension fund Ilmarinen joins IQM's expanded $146 million PIPE financing alongside their SPAC merger. The upsized funding round demonstrates that major institutional investors are increasingly willing to back quantum computing companies through the challenging path from R&D to commercialization, particularly those like IQM that focus on full-stack superconducting quantum systems. While SPAC deals in quantum have had mixed results, the additional commitment from a major Finnish pension fund suggests sophisticated investors see real value in IQM's European quantum computing approach and customer traction. This funding boost should help IQM accelerate their hardware development and compete more effectively with established players like IBM and emerging rivals in the increasingly crowded quantum hardware space.

Insider Brief IQM Finland Oy, a global leader in full-stack superconducting quantum computers (“IQM”, “IQM Quantum Computers” or the “Company”) and Real Asset Acquisition Corp. (Nasdaq: RAAQ), a special purpose acquisition company (“RAAQ”), today announced an additional PIPE commitment from Ilmarinen in connection with the previously announced business combination between IQM and RAAQ. Ilmarinen’s new commitment comes […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Xanadu Launches Public Cloud Access for Borealis
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a significant milestone as Xanadu becomes the first company to offer public cloud access to a quantum computer that has demonstrated quantum computational advantage, breaking the traditional barrier between research achievements and practical accessibility. The Borealis system's photonic architecture represents a compelling alternative to the superconducting and trapped-ion approaches that dominate current quantum cloud offerings, potentially offering advantages in operating temperature and network connectivity for future quantum internet applications. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the full programmability of all gates, giving researchers unprecedented flexibility to explore quantum algorithms on a platform that has already proven its computational superiority over classical systems. This democratization of quantum advantage hardware could accelerate algorithm development and bring us closer to discovering the first commercially viable quantum applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Xanadu has demonstrated quantum computational advantage using Borealis, their newest photonic quantum computer. It is the first photonic quantum computer offering full programmability of all its gates to demonstrate quantum computational advantage, and the first time that a machine capable of quantum advantage has been made available to the public […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Finland Launches QScale Project to Advance Quantum Computing Scalability
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Finnish initiative represents a crucial step toward solving one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges - scaling up while maintaining energy efficiency and signal precision. The QScale project's focus on optical control and ultra-precise signal technology addresses the fundamental bottleneck that has limited quantum computers to relatively small qubit counts, as traditional electronic control systems become increasingly complex and error-prone at scale. With Finland's strong track record in quantum research and VTT's expertise in photonics, this collaboration could yield breakthrough approaches that make fault-tolerant quantum computers with thousands of qubits more feasible. The project's emphasis on energy efficiency is particularly timely, as the quantum computing field grapples with the environmental impact of scaling up these inherently power-hungry systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The QScale joint project coordinated by VTT with Tampere and Aalto Universities has received funding from Business Finland’s prestigious Rise to Challenge Programme. In the project, researchers are developing a radical improvement in the energy efficiency and scalability of quantum computers through optical control and ultra-precise signal technology based on […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantinuum Expands IPO as Valuation Climbs Above $14 Billion
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a potential watershed moment for the quantum computing industry, as Quantinuum's IPO could become the largest public market debut in the sector's history with a valuation exceeding $14 billion. The strong investor demand that prompted the company to expand its share offering from the original plan signals growing mainstream confidence in quantum computing's commercial viability, particularly significant given current market conditions. Quantinuum's public listing would provide crucial visibility into the financial realities of quantum hardware companies and could pave the way for other quantum firms to access public markets. The timing is notable as it comes amid increased competition in quantum computing hardware and growing enterprise interest in quantum cloud services.

Insider Brief Quantinuum is increasing the size of its initial public offering as investor demand continues to build ahead of what could become the largest public-market debut in the history of the quantum computing sector. According to Barron’s, Quantinuum now plans to offer 26.5 million shares priced between $53 and $55 each, up from an […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Conversation: Quantum Computers Could Expose Our Digital Secrets – But there are Much Better Reasons to Build them
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most persistent misconceptions about quantum computing - that breaking encryption is the primary or only reason to develop these machines. While quantum computers will indeed pose challenges to current cryptographic systems, this piece from Royal Holloway researchers emphasizes the far more exciting potential applications, from drug discovery and materials science to optimization problems that could revolutionize logistics and finance. The timing is particularly relevant as we're seeing increased investment in quantum-safe cryptography alongside rapid progress in quantum hardware, making it crucial for the public to understand that quantum computing's true promise extends far beyond cybersecurity concerns. This balanced perspective helps counter the often sensationalized "quantum apocalypse" narrative while highlighting why quantum computing represents one of the most transformative technologies of our time.

By Keith Martin, Professor, Information Security Group, Royal Holloway, University of London and Briana Bowen, Postgraduate research student, Department of Information Security, Royal Holloway, University of London for The Conversation Quantum computers are coming. Or, at least, that’s what current predictions say. These machines harness the power of quantum mechanics, the set of rules governing […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: At Its First NYSE Investor Day, D-Wave Bets on Two Quantum Machines, Not One
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights D-Wave's strategic pivot toward a dual-technology approach, combining their established quantum annealing systems with gate-based quantum computers to address different problem types. D-Wave's CEO taking the unusual step of encouraging investor skepticism demonstrates remarkable transparency in an industry often criticized for overhyped promises, potentially setting a new standard for how quantum companies communicate with stakeholders. The timing of this investor day at the NYSE signals D-Wave's confidence in their commercial viability as they position themselves to compete across multiple quantum computing paradigms. This dual-machine strategy could prove crucial as the quantum industry matures and different applications require specialized quantum architectures.

Insider Brief On June 1, D-Wave Quantum held its first investor day at the New York Stock Exchange under the banner “The D-Wave Difference.” Alan Baratz, CEO of D-Wave, opened by urging the room to be skeptical of quantum computing companies, his own included. Quantum mechanics is hard, the chief executive said, and that makes […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Amazon Braket launches Rigetti Cepheus™-1-108Q superconducting device
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement marks a significant milestone in making high-quality quantum hardware more accessible, as Amazon Braket now offers Rigetti's latest 108-qubit superconducting processor to cloud users worldwide. The Cepheus-1-108Q represents a substantial leap in qubit count for Rigetti's publicly available systems, potentially enabling researchers to explore quantum algorithms and applications that require larger, more complex quantum circuits than previously possible through cloud platforms. This expansion of Braket's hardware portfolio is particularly noteworthy because it democratizes access to cutting-edge quantum processors, allowing smaller research groups and companies to experiment with near-term quantum computing without the massive infrastructure investments typically required. The availability of diverse quantum hardware architectures through a single cloud interface continues to accelerate the pace of quantum algorithm development and benchmarking across the field.

This post was contributed to by Zia Mohammad, Scott Smart, Yuvraj Mohan, Mike Piech, and Rebecca Malamud Figure 1: A Rigetti quantum processing unit (QPU) inside a dilution refrigerator. Photo by Drew Bird. Amazon Braket enables customers to design and run quantum algorithms on a broad selection of quantum hardware through a unified interface. On […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Matter may entangle with light far more easily near quantum critical points
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's biggest challenges: scaling entanglement from tiny laboratory systems to large-scale materials that could enable practical quantum technologies. The discovery that quantum critical points - special phase transitions where materials hover between different quantum states - dramatically enhance matter-light entanglement could unlock new pathways for quantum communication and sensing applications. By identifying conditions where macroscopic quantum effects naturally emerge, Si's work bridges the gap between fundamental quantum physics and engineering quantum devices that work at room temperature and larger scales. This finding could accelerate development of quantum networks that rely on strong matter-light interactions, potentially making quantum internet infrastructure more feasible.

Quantum entanglement is a state in which particles are entwined with each other. In this entwined state, the properties of one particle influence the other, even when they aren't physically close to each other. This phenomenon has often been observed in small quantum systems with only a few particles in them, where researchers can use it to store and process quantum information. Rice University professor Qimiao Si is interested in understanding and applying quantum entanglement to macroscopic systems with vast numbers of particles.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Simulation of Nucleon-Antinucleon Interaction in Large-$N$ QCD$_2$ on an IBM Quantum Nighthawk Processor
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article demonstrates a fascinating bridge between quantum field theory and practical quantum computing by simulating the fundamental interaction between matter and antimatter particles using IBM's quantum hardware. The researchers cleverly mapped a complex problem in quantum chromodynamics - specifically how nucleons and antinucleons interact - onto a spin chain model that could be implemented on current noisy quantum devices. What makes this particularly impressive is their use of structured error cancellation techniques that allowed them to extract meaningful physics results despite the inherent noise in today's quantum processors. This work showcases how quantum computers are beginning to tackle genuine problems in high-energy physics that are computationally intractable for classical computers, potentially opening new avenues for understanding the fundamental forces of nature.

We report a quantum simulation of the nucleon--antinucleon interaction in large-$N$ two-dimensional quantum chromodynamics (QCD$_2$) on the IBM Quantum Nighthawk processor. In the large-$N$ limit, QCD$_2$ admits a bosonized description in which baryons emerge as topological solitons (kinks) of an effective mesonic field theory, providing a controlled, nonperturbative framework for baryon--antibaryon dynamics. We formulate the problem by mapping the continuum bosonized Hamiltonian to a spin-chain representation equivalent to an XXZ model with anisotropy set by the QCD parameters. In this mapping, nucleon and antinucleon states correspond to kink and antikink excitations, respectively, while their interaction is encoded in the spin correlations of the chain. Using Jordan--Wigner encoding, we implement the resulting XXZ Hamiltonian on a finite set of qubits and realize it via a variational ground state ansatz and postselected nonunitary disorder operator insertions optimized for the Nighthawk architecture. We then show the kink--antikink interaction potential built from the conditional energies of these nonunitary string operators can be robustly extracted from the quantum hardware due to structured error cancelation. The resulting potential exhibits the expected attractive behavior. The quantum simulation results are benchmarked against exact diagonalization, ideal statevector evaluation showing good agreement. To connect the device result to the continuum field theory, we extract the potential in the continuum limit using large-$L$ matrix product state calculations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Chutes and Ladders: Dynamical Automorphisms via the ZX-Calculus
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough in quantum error correction by extending the versatile ZX-calculus graphical language to handle dynamical stabilizer codes, opening new pathways for implementing logical operations that were previously impossible. The researchers demonstrate their approach by successfully implementing a logical phase gate in the seven-qubit "bare code" - a significant achievement since this code has no non-trivial logical operations available through conventional single-qubit operations. The "chutes and ladders" metaphor captures how their method creates shortcuts through the space of stabilizer codes via measurement-based code switching, essentially finding clever detours around the limitations of static error correction approaches. This work could unlock more flexible and efficient quantum error correction schemes by making previously inaccessible logical gates implementable through dynamical code switching rather than brute-force circuit compilation.

The ZX-calculus is a powerful graphical language for manipulating quantum circuits, which has recently found many applications in quantum error correction. We extend this language to handle Floquet and other dynamical stabilizer codes via the connection between measurement-based code switching and gauge fixing (arXiv:1810.10037). We combine gauge-fixing steps to implement a closed loop in the space of stabilizer codes, returning to the original codespace up to a logical Clifford gate. These measurement-based paths in the space of stabilizer codes can be viewed as shortcuts, or "chutes and ladders", relative to single-qubit Clifford operations and qubit permutations. This yields a machine-interpretable method for constructing dynamical automorphisms and facilitates the search for implementations of desired logical gates. As an example, we implement a logical phase gate via distance-preserving code switching for the seven-qubit code bare code (arXiv:1702.01155), which has no non-trivial logical Clifford gates based on single-qubit Clifford operations and qubit permutations (arXiv:2409.18175).

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Hybrid Clifford Codes via Operator Algebra Quantum Error Correction and Projective Representation Theory
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work represents a significant theoretical advance in quantum error correction by extending Clifford codes—already a powerful generalization of stabilizer codes—into new hybrid territories that can simultaneously protect both classical and quantum information. The authors' use of operator algebra quantum error correction and projective representation theory opens up previously unexplored code families, potentially offering new pathways to more efficient error correction schemes that could handle mixed classical-quantum computational tasks. While highly theoretical, this mathematical framework could prove crucial for future quantum systems that need to process both types of information simultaneously, such as quantum-classical hybrid algorithms or quantum networking protocols. The extension beyond traditional stabilizer codes is particularly noteworthy, as it suggests these new hybrid codes might offer protection capabilities that conventional approaches cannot achieve.

Clifford codes are a natural generalization of quantum stabilizer codes based primarily on representation theory. This class of codes has previously been extended to the setting of quantum subsystem codes. We formulate a two-fold generalization of Clifford codes, for both the hybrid classical and quantum information and projective representation theory settings. This leads to new classes of hybrid subspace and subsystem Clifford codes. We extend the fundamental representation theoretic quantum error correction theorem to include these codes, based on the operator algebra quantum error correction framework. We also discuss several examples throughout the presentation, of both stabilizer and non-stabilizer type.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Multidimensional Reconciliation in Continuous-Variable QKD: Review, Coding Schemes, and Open Source Simulation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive review tackles one of the most critical bottlenecks in continuous-variable quantum key distribution: the reconciliation process that extracts secure keys from noisy quantum measurements. The work's exploration of high-dimensional reconciliation schemes beyond traditional low dimensions could significantly extend the practical range and efficiency of CV-QKD systems, which currently struggle with the noise and losses inherent in real-world fiber networks. Perhaps most valuable to the field is the release of HDirac, an open-source simulation framework that will enable researchers worldwide to experiment with and optimize multidimensional reconciliation techniques without starting from scratch. This type of accessible tooling often accelerates practical quantum cryptography development by lowering barriers for academic labs and industry teams working on next-generation secure communication systems.

Continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD) requires highly efficient reconciliation techniques to operate at low signal-to-noise ratios and long distances. Multidimensional reconciliation addresses this challenge by transforming the physical Gaussian quantum channel into a virtual binary-input additive white Gaussian noise (BIAWGN) channel, enabling the use of modern errorcorrecting codes. In this work, we review the principles of multidimensional reconciliation, with a particular focus on high-dimensional constructions beyond the algebraic dimensions 1, 2, 4, 8. We describe the construction of the virtual channel, discuss practical coding schemes for reverse reconciliation, and analyse their integration with linear error-correcting codes. We also present an opensource simulation framework, HDirac, implementing multidimensional reconciliation for arbitrary dimensions, and use it to evaluate state-of-the-art LDPC codes. The results highlight key trade-offs between dimension, reconciliation efficiency, and frame error rate, providing practical guidance for CV-QKD system design.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Is the most random pattern random? Maximizing localization in a two-dimensional lattice with engineered disorder
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research flips the conventional approach to Anderson localization on its head by deliberately engineering disorder patterns rather than relying on random ones, potentially opening new pathways for controlling quantum states in 2D systems. The finding that carefully designed disorder can achieve significantly better localization than random patterns could have immediate applications in quantum processor design, particularly for minimizing unwanted qubit interactions during idle states. What makes this especially intriguing is the connection between spatial disorder patterns and localization efficiency, suggesting that quantum engineers might be able to design specific lattice configurations that naturally suppress decoherence. While the full qubit lattice simulations remain computationally challenging, the demonstrated transferability from single-particle models to quantum systems provides a practical roadmap for implementing these optimization strategies on near-term quantum devices.

We investigate localization in two models: a single particle in a two-dimensional square lattice described by the tight binding Hamiltonian, and a two-dimensional square qubit lattice. It is well-known that Anderson localization occurs under suitable conditions in which the system parameters are chosen randomly from some statistical distribution. We propose a situation in which the parameters, specifically the on-site energies, are carefully chosen in such a way that a localization-quantifying parameter is maximized. We demonstrate the optimization procedure with numerical calculations in which the engineered localization significantly exceeds the average localization caused by a random distribution of the on-site energies. We explore the relation between spatial patterns and localization efficiency. Furthermore, we use perturbation theory to gain insight into the localization mechanism and obtain an improved cost function for optimization calculations, leading to enhanced localization in both the single-particle and full Hilbert spaces. Although large-scale simulations for qubit lattices are computationally infeasible, we use small-system simulations to demonstrate that results obtained using the single-particle tight binding model can be adapted to identify optimal settings for qubit lattice systems to achieve maximum decoupling between the qubits, which can be valuable for optimizing the idle-state settings on a quantum processor.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum optimal control of the Dicke manifold in Rydberg atom arrays
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most fundamental challenges in quantum computing: how to control large quantum systems without getting lost in the exponentially growing complexity of their quantum states. The researchers developed a clever mathematical trick called "irrep distillation" that focuses specifically on symmetric quantum states (Dicke states) while accounting for unwanted leakage effects that plague real Rydberg atom systems. What makes this particularly exciting is that they've created practical control protocols that work even without precise individual atom addressing - a significant engineering constraint - while still achieving high-fidelity preparation of useful quantum states like GHZ states. This work represents a crucial step toward making neutral atom quantum computers more practical by providing robust control methods that can handle the messy realities of dipole-dipole interactions in large atomic arrays.

The ability to engineer and control quantum states of many-body systems is a central challenge in quantum information science. For a register of $N$ qubits, the full Hilbert space dimension grows exponentially as $2^N$, rendering generic state preparation and control infeasible without exploiting structure or symmetry. A particularly important and physically motivated restriction is to the fully symmetric subspace, spanned by the Dicke states, which are simultaneous eigenstates of collective spin $J=N/2$. Ensembles of Rydberg atoms interacting via electric dipoles in two-dimensional tweezer arrays form a promising platform for achieving such control. However, the finite range of dipole-dipole interactions poses a challenge to generating and controlling the Dicke manifold because the Hamiltonian incurs leakage from the computational subspace. To counteract this leakage, we perform quantum optimal control algorithms on a truncated Hilbert space according to our newly developed method of ``irrep distillation'' (IRD), which captures the process by which the symmetric subspace couples to leakage error-spaces, using only linear-scaling Hilbert dimension. We implement gradient ascent pulse engineering (GrAPE) on control schemes with little or no local addressing, to generate resourceful states like Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger, Dicke, and extremal quantum states. We benchmark each scheme of IRD-GrAPE for its quantum speed limit (QSL), as well as exactly testing pulse fidelities on small system sizes and predicting fidelities using higher-order IRD on larger systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Optimized Point Addition Circuits for Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research addresses a critical gap in quantum cryptanalysis by providing the detailed circuit implementations that previous work kept under wraps, essentially giving the cryptographic community the blueprints for how Shor's algorithm could efficiently break elliptic curve cryptography. The authors achieve a meaningful optimization trade-off, reducing gate counts by up to 10% while using only 1.5% more qubits compared to recent improvements, which matters enormously when every quantum gate represents a potential point of failure in noisy quantum systems. What makes this particularly significant is the focus on secp256k1, the elliptic curve that secures Bitcoin and many other cryptocurrencies, providing concrete cost estimates for breaking real-world cryptographic systems. This transparency in circuit design accelerates both the timeline for assessing quantum threats and the development of post-quantum cryptographic defenses.

Shor's algorithm represents the main threat of quantum computers to cryptography. In order to precisely understand its feasibility, many authors have worked towards reducing its costs, either at the logical level (assuming a fault-tolerant architecture), or at the physical level (taking into account the constraints of envisioned hardware). In particular, recent works by Chevignard et al. (CRYPTO 2024) and Gidney (arXiv 2025) used improved arithmetic to significantly reduce the qubit cost of factoring RSA public keys. Even more recently, Babbush et al. (arXiv 2026) improved the cost of computing elliptic curve discrete logarithms, with a reduction of a factor 2 to 3 in gate count and qubit count compared to a previous work by Litinski (arXiv 2023). Their result relies on optimized point addition circuits on elliptic curves over prime fields. However they did not reveal their logical quantum circuits, relying instead on a zero-knowledge proof. In this paper, we detail a quantum logical circuit architecture which gives similar results as Babbush et al., with a slightly higher number of qubits (around 1.5% increase) and a slightly smaller Toffoli gate count (between 6.5% and 10% reduction) for the curve secp256k1. We also give gate counts for a generic variant of the circuit, which is valid for any prime field.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Fixstars Amplify Adds QUDORA’s Quantum Computers to its Standard Machines
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant step in making quantum computing more accessible to Japanese enterprises, as Fixstars Amplify's optimization platform now integrates QUDORA's quantum cloud services for the first time in Japan. The collaboration is particularly noteworthy because it bridges the gap between quantum hardware and practical optimization applications, allowing businesses to leverage quantum algorithms through Amplify's user-friendly interface without needing deep quantum expertise. This type of platform integration is becoming increasingly important as the quantum industry matures, enabling companies to experiment with quantum optimization solutions for real-world problems like logistics, scheduling, and resource allocation. The move also highlights the growing trend of quantum startups partnering with established software platforms to accelerate commercial adoption of quantum technologies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Fixstars Amplify Corporation (President and CEO: Yoshiki Matsuda) has signed a partnership agreement with the German quantum startup QUDORA Technologies GmbH (hereinafter “QUDORA”)—the first of its kind in Japan – and has added QUDORA’s quantum computing environment, “QUDORA Cloud,” to the optimization cloud service “Fixstars Amplify” (hereinafter “Amplify”) as a […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: D-Wave’s New Gate-Model Roadmap Puts Pin in 2032 For 100 Logical-Qubit System
item.description:
Curator's Take: D-Wave's ambitious 2032 timeline for a 100-logical-qubit system represents a significant strategic shift for the company, which has traditionally dominated the quantum annealing space but is now making serious moves into gate-model quantum computing where IBM and Google currently lead. This roadmap puts D-Wave in direct competition with the major players' fault-tolerance milestones, with 100 logical qubits representing a crucial threshold where quantum computers could begin solving commercially relevant problems that classical computers cannot handle efficiently. The announcement signals D-Wave's recognition that while quantum annealing has its niche applications, the broader quantum computing market will likely be won by whoever achieves practical fault-tolerant gate-model systems first. What makes this particularly interesting is D-Wave's unique position as the only company pursuing both quantum paradigms simultaneously, potentially giving them valuable diversification as the field evolves.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE: QBTS), (“D-Wave” or the “Company”), the only dual-platform quantum computing company providing both annealing and gate-model systems, software and services, today announced a new gate-model roadmap designed to accelerate the development of commercial, fault-tolerant quantum computing. Targeting 100 logical qubits capable of successfully performing over one […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Penalty-free quantum optimization applied to lattice protein folding
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental challenge in quantum optimization by eliminating the penalty terms that typically plague quantum algorithms when solving constrained problems like protein folding. By cleverly reformulating the lattice protein folding problem as a maximum independent set search and using a specialized QAOA mixer, the team avoids the quadratic penalty functions that often dominate the optimization landscape and lead to poor performance. The penalty-free approach not only produces cleaner objective functions but also enables the algorithm to scale to 14-amino acid proteins using just 26 qubits through an innovative iterative local-search scheme. This work represents a significant methodological advance that could be applied to many other constrained optimization problems in quantum computing, potentially unlocking better performance across a wide range of real-world applications.

Identifying minimum-energy structures of lattice proteins is a challenging discrete optimization problem. Quantum approaches such as analog quantum annealing and the gate-based quantum approximate optimization algorithm (QAOA) can address this problem after mapping it to a binary representation, which typically involves introducing penalty terms to enforce valid chain configurations. However, in this and many related problems, the use of quadratic penalty terms can be avoided by restricting the search space to independent sets in a conflict graph and using a QAOA mixer designed for the maximum independent set problem. In this work, we implement and explore this QAOA variant for lattice protein folding. Here, the objective function consists solely of the protein energy together with a simple linear bias term, without quadratic penalties. We validate this approach through classical simulations of the quantum circuits for lattice proteins of lengths $N=4$ and $N=6$. To explore larger systems, we further introduce a heuristic iterative local-search scheme, with which we successfully fold lattice proteins with lengths up to $N=14$ using local subgraphs with at most 26 qubits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: How a New Phase of Matter Could Impact Quantum Technology
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant leap forward in materials science that could unlock new possibilities for quantum technologies. By successfully stabilizing a theoretically predicted but previously elusive phase of matter using precisely engineered nanoparticle superlattices, the Brown and Michigan teams have opened a pathway to materials with potentially exotic quantum properties. The ability to freeze and control these fleeting structural phases could lead to quantum materials with tailored properties for applications ranging from quantum sensors to quantum computers, where controlling matter at the nanoscale is crucial for maintaining delicate quantum states. This work demonstrates how the convergence of nanotechnology and quantum physics continues to push the boundaries of what's possible in quantum device engineering.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Using finely tuned nanoscale building blocks, researchers from Brown University and the University of Michigan College of Engineering have stabilized a fleeting structural phase of matter that had been predicted theoretically but never before stabilized in a physical material. The new nanoparticle superlattice, described in the journal Science, freezes an elusive […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Lie Algebra-Based Quantum Optimal Controls Interpolation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's most persistent bottlenecks: the computational nightmare of optimizing control pulses for complex quantum operations in real-time. By combining elegant mathematics from Lie group theory with machine learning, the team has created a "universal translator" that can instantly generate the precise electromagnetic pulses needed to execute any quantum operation on superconducting qubits, without having to solve optimization problems from scratch each time. The breakthrough is particularly exciting for quantum simulation applications like modeling neutrino oscillations, where researchers need to rapidly execute thousands of different quantum operations - a task that would normally require prohibitive computational overhead. This approach could dramatically accelerate the pace of quantum algorithm development by making it feasible to run complex simulations that were previously computationally impractical.

We present a framework combining Lie group theory and feed-forward neural networks to efficiently generate quantum optimal control pulses for arbitrary unitary operations in superconducting qubit systems, bypassing the need for explicit optimization at inference time. The exponential scaling of the Hilbert space dimension with qubit number makes standard optimization approaches computationally prohibitive when large ensembles of distinct propagators must be processed, a bottleneck that is particularly acute in Trotterized quantum simulation. Our method addresses this limitation by pre-computing a representative set of control pulses via Lie group theory and training neural networks to map target propagators to their corresponding pulses. We demonstrate the approach on superconducting qubit systems of 2, 3, and 4 qubits, finding high reconstruction fidelity for specific combinations of Lie algebra parameters. As a physically motivated benchmark, we apply the methodology to reconstruct control pulses for the Trotter propagators of a neutrino system undergoing collective flavor oscillations. The successful generalization across system types demonstrates that a single model -- trained once on hardware-specific random propagators -- can serve as a universal control-pulse generator for any target quantum system of compatible Hilbert space dimension, offering a promising route toward scalable quantum simulation.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum resonance encryption for secure data storage and communication with quantum kicked top
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a fascinating approach to quantum data protection using quantum kicked tops - essentially spinning quantum systems that can create complex, chaotic dynamics when operated at specific resonance conditions. The protocol addresses a critical real-world problem: how to keep your data private even from the quantum cloud service provider itself, which becomes increasingly important as quantum computing moves toward shared, cloud-based access models. What makes this particularly clever is that authorized users can perfectly recover their data while any unauthorized access attempts only see scrambled, mixed quantum states that reveal nothing useful. While the full protocol requires mature quantum networks that don't exist yet, the researchers note it can be demonstrated on current quantum computing platforms, making this a bridge between today's capabilities and tomorrow's quantum internet security needs.

In a shared quantum computer, how to ensure data privacy and protection from access by unauthorized parties? We propose a genuine quantum protocol for protecting user's data which is not accessible even to the service provider. The protocol is based on quantum kicked top -- the dynamics of a spin system --operating at quantum resonance regime. This protocol ensures perfect recovery for authorized users while making intercepted states appear mixed to eavesdroppers, with built-in tampering detection. This protocol can also be used for secure communication between two parties in geographically different locations, and also for quantum key distribution. The effectiveness of this protocol is demonstrated by assuming a quantum computer with quantum memory and functioning quantum networks. In the absence of the latter, at present, the protocol can be demonstrated in laboratory using currently available quantum computing platforms.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: An Explicit Scott-Type Bound for Absolutely Maximally Entangled States with Arbitrary Defect
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a fundamental theoretical problem that has major implications for quantum error correction and secure quantum communication protocols. The researchers have solved a long-standing conjecture by providing explicit mathematical bounds on when certain highly entangled quantum states can exist, extending previous work that only covered specific cases to now handle arbitrary "defects" in the entanglement structure. These bounds are crucial because absolutely maximally entangled states serve as the building blocks for quantum secret sharing schemes and quantum error-correcting codes, so understanding their fundamental limitations helps determine what's theoretically possible in quantum information processing. The work provides concrete mathematical tools that could guide the design of more efficient quantum protocols by clearly delineating the boundaries between what's achievable and what's fundamentally impossible in multipartite quantum systems.

Absolutely maximally entangled (AME) states and, more generally, $k$-uniform states in $(\C^q)^{\otimes n}$ are central objects in multipartite entanglement theory, with applications to quantum secret sharing, quantum masking, and quantum error correction. In the extremal case $k=\lfloor n/2\rfloor$, Scott (2004) proved a sharp nonexistence bound showing that AME states cannot exist once the number of parties $n$ exceeds a threshold of order $2q^{2}$ (with a parity dependence on $n$), where $q$ is the local dimension. Recently, Ning et al.\ studied \emph{defective} AME states (i.e., $k=\lfloor n/2\rfloor-l$ with $l>0$), gave explicit Scott-type bounds for defects $l=1,2$ and conjectured a general $(2l+2)q^{2}+o(q^{2})$ behavior. In this paper, we solve this conjecture and establish a fully explicit Scott-type upper bound for AME states with arbitrary defect $l\ge 0$, yielding Scott's bound for $l=0$ and Ning et al.'s bounds for $l=1,2$ as special cases. Equivalently, this gives nonexistence bounds for one-dimensional pure quantum error-correcting codes near the quantum Singleton regime. The proof uses a truncated MacWilliams linear-programming system and an explicit infeasibility certificate. As a direct application, we derive explicit asymptotic upper bounds on $k/n$ for fixed local dimension $q$, improving the implicit upper bounds given by Ning et al.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Half the Interference, Most of the Answer: Approximate Quantum Simulation via Path-Sum Pruning
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a fascinating and underexplored bottleneck in quantum simulation: the computational cost of interference itself, not just the exponential state space that gets all the attention. By treating quantum interference as a separate, schedulable computation using a chemistry-inspired model, the researchers demonstrate that you can skip nearly half of the interference calculations while maintaining over 90% accuracy on key quantum algorithms like Grover search and Shor's algorithm. While this doesn't solve the fundamental exponential scaling problem, it reveals important insights about the structure of quantum interference that could inform both classical simulation strategies and potentially even quantum algorithm design. The work opens up intriguing questions about which aspects of quantum interference are truly essential for extracting useful computational results versus which are redundant overhead.

Classical simulation of quantum circuits is expensive for two distinct reasons. The obvious one is state-space size: an n-qubit system requires exponentially many amplitudes. The less obvious one is interference: useful output distributions emerge only after many computational histories have been coherently combined at common endpoints, and this aggregation step is itself a substantial source of cost. We introduce statistical interference sampling, a framework that makes this second bottleneck explicit by treating endpoint interference as a separately schedulable computation. Using the Chemical Abstract Machine (ChAM) as our model, weighted path contributions evolve as concurrent molecular species, and interference reactions combine contributions that share a common output state. A threshold rule terminates the process once an endpoint accumulates sufficient amplitude, discarding the remaining reactions. The method does not improve worst-case complexity and is not intended as a general-purpose simulator. Its purpose is to ask a more targeted question: how much of the interference calculation can be skipped while still recovering a useful output distribution? On benchmark circuits for Deutsch-Jozsa, Grover search, Simon's problem, and small Shor period-finding instances, we find that nearly 50% of endpoint interference reactions can be omitted while maintaining over 90% output accuracy for most algorithms tested. These results suggest that interference arithmetic is a structured resource that admits meaningful approximation, and that exposing it explicitly opens new opportunities for pruning strategies across path-sum, Pauli-path, and tensor-network simulation methods.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SEALSQ Deepens Partnership with Quantum Computing Startup EeroQ
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership highlights the growing convergence between quantum computing hardware development and the urgent need for quantum-resistant cybersecurity solutions. SEALSQ's investment in EeroQ's electron-on-helium quantum architecture is particularly noteworthy because this CMOS-compatible approach could potentially offer a more practical path to scalable quantum processors compared to traditional superconducting or trapped-ion systems. The collaboration makes strategic sense as SEALSQ's expertise in post-quantum cryptography pairs naturally with EeroQ's novel quantum computing platform, potentially creating an integrated solution that addresses both sides of the quantum revolution. While electron-on-helium qubits remain relatively early-stage compared to other quantum technologies, this partnership signals serious industry investment in alternative quantum computing approaches that could prove more manufacturable using existing semiconductor infrastructure.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SEALSQ Corp (NASDAQ: LAES) (“SEALSQ” or the “Company”), a global leader in semiconductors, PKI, and post-quantum cybersecurity technologies, today announced a further strategic investment in EeroQ, the U.S.-based quantum chip design company pioneering a patented, breakthrough, and fully CMOS compatible quantum computing architecture based on electrons on helium (eHe). Building on the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Coherent Exchange and Decoherence in Dirac-Spin-Liquid Quantum Interconnects
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant theoretical framework for understanding how exotic quantum spin liquids could serve as "quantum buses" to connect and entangle distant qubits, potentially offering a new pathway for quantum computing architectures. The researchers show that the same physical property of the spin liquid - its susceptibility - governs both the useful coherent coupling between qubits and the harmful decoherence, creating a fundamental trade-off that determines whether such systems could be practically viable. What makes this particularly exciting is that it provides concrete theoretical tools to evaluate real candidate materials like Dirac spin liquids, moving beyond abstract concepts toward potentially implementable quantum interconnects. The work bridges cutting-edge condensed matter physics with quantum information science, suggesting that some of the most exotic states of matter discovered in recent years might find applications in future quantum technologies.

We develop a susceptibility-based open-system theory for two localized qubits coupled through a candidate two-dimensional $\mathrm{U}(1)$ Dirac-spin-liquid-like bath. The central input is the gauge-invariant retarded physical spin susceptibility $\Chi^R(q,ω)$ of the bath. We show that this single response kernel controls both coherent and dissipative qubit dynamics: its real part generates the nonlocal mediated exchange, while its absorptive part determines relaxation and dephasing through the equilibrium noise spectrum. This gives a unified reduced two-qubit description in which the usefulness of the bath as an entanglement bus is governed by the competition between susceptibility-mediated exchange and bath-induced decoherence. As an analytically transparent benchmark, we evaluate the spinon mean-field Dirac susceptibility and recover the static algebraic exchange $J_{\mathrm{eff}}(R)\propto J_{\rm local}^2/(v_F R^3)$, together with pseudogap-suppressed relaxation $Γ_1\propto J_{\rm local}^2ω_0^3/v_F^4$. We then formulate a beyond-mean-field extension in which gauge-field dressing and other interaction effects are absorbed into a dressed physical susceptibility, without changing the reduced qubit-sector mapping. The resulting framework provides a direct route from the many-body spin response of a correlated two-dimensional bath to reduced-dynamics simulations of entanglement generation, coherence loss, and the operational phase space of a candidate Dirac spin-liquid quantum interconnect.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum light source with lithium tantalate for scalable photonic quantum circuits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough marks the first successful quantum light source built on thin-film lithium tantalate, a material platform that has already proven highly successful for classical photonic applications. The achievement is particularly significant because it bridges the gap between mature classical photonic manufacturing and quantum photonics, potentially offering a path to mass-producible quantum devices using existing industrial fabrication techniques. The demonstrated source produces high-quality entangled photon pairs in the telecom band with excellent performance metrics, including strong antibunching and Bell-inequality-violating entanglement visibility above 92%. By establishing compatibility between quantum functionality and a manufacturing-ready platform, this work could accelerate the development of practical photonic quantum computing systems that leverage both classical and quantum optical components on the same chip.

Thin-film lithium tantalate (TFLT) has emerged as a promising integrated photonic platform owing to its low photorefractive noise, high optical damage threshold, and reduced birefringence, attracting increasing interest for scalable photonic technologies. Here, to the best of our knowledge, we demonstrate the first quantum light source with TFLT via spontaneous four-wave mixing, bridging the gap between the rapidly advancing classical TFLT ecosystem and integrated quantum photonics. The fabricated microring exhibits a free spectral range of 350~GHz and an optical quality factor of $10^6$, enabling efficient cavity-enhanced nonlinear interactions. Correlated photon pairs are generated across the telecom band from 1510 to 1570~nm, with a photon pair generation rate of 24 $\mathrm{MHz/mW^{2}}$ at a wavelength of 1535.04 nm. The source delivers strongly antibunched heralded single photons with $g^{(2)}_{H}(0)=0.071\pm0.004$ at a heralding rate of 170 kHz, while the unheralded statistics yield $g^{(2)}(0)=1.93 \pm 0.05$, indicating near-single-temporal-mode emission. Energy-time entanglement is further confirmed by a raw two-photon interference visibility of $92.55\pm0.94\%$, well above the Bell-inequality violation threshold. These results establish TFLT as a manufacturing-compatible platform for scalable photonic quantum circuits, paving the way for the monolithic co-integration of classical and quantum photonic functionalities.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Extensible Fluxonium Architecture Using Tunable Couplers with Low Shunt Capacitance
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing challenges in quantum computing: scaling up fluxonium qubits, which have shown exceptional performance in small systems but struggled with integration into large 2D arrays. The breakthrough lies in developing tunable couplers with low shunt capacitance that can connect multiple fluxonium qubits while maintaining their superior coherence properties and enabling fast, high-fidelity gates. This work represents a significant step toward making fluxonium-based quantum computers viable at scale, potentially offering an alternative pathway to the transmon-dominated landscape with better error rates and longer qubit lifetimes. The proposed architecture could reshape how we think about building large-scale quantum processors, especially as the field pushes toward fault-tolerant systems where every improvement in qubit quality matters exponentially.

Fluxonium qubits have demonstrated high-fidelity operations and long coherence times in small-scale systems, highlighting their promise for quantum computing. However, large-scale integration into a high-performance two-dimensional (2D) qubit array remains the central challenge for practical applications. In this work, we introduce an extensible architecture for scaling up fluxonium qubits in 2D grids. To address the key challenges, namely achieving controllable strong interaction and high connectivity for qubits featuring small shunting capacitors (footprints), we propose using low-shunt-capacitance couplers to enable tunable interactions between fluxonium qubits. When embedded into 2D square lattices, large couplings can be achieved even with relatively small coupling capacitances, thus enabling multiple connections with sufficient capacitance budget. We further propose coupler realizations based on generalized flux qubit circuits, specifically the quarton and the fluxonium, and demonstrate that both enable fast, high-fidelity gates with low spectator errors, while supporting multiple connections on 2D grids.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: French National Quantum Update: May 2026
item.description:
Curator's Take: This substantial €1.55 billion commitment from France signals a major escalation in the global quantum race, positioning the country as a serious contender alongside established quantum powers like the US and China. The timing is particularly strategic as European nations increasingly recognize quantum computing as critical infrastructure for future economic competitiveness and national security. France's coordinated approach, combining significant capital investment with alignment to broader European industrial policy, could accelerate the development of a truly competitive European quantum ecosystem. This level of government backing provides the long-term funding stability that quantum hardware companies desperately need to bridge the gap between current noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and fault-tolerant quantum computers.

Executive Brief France’s quantum sector is entering a more heavily capitalized and strategically coordinated phase, led by a major expansion of the country’s national investment agenda and deeper alignment with European industrial policy. In May 2026, French President Emmanuel Macron announced an additional €1 billion commitment to France’s national quantum plan alongside €550 million earmarked […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Who’s News: Strategic Leadership Appointments at Quandela, Zapata Quantum, Imperagen, and EigenQ
item.description:
Curator's Take: These strategic leadership appointments signal a critical maturation phase for the quantum computing industry, as companies transition from research-focused startups to commercially-viable enterprises requiring seasoned operational expertise. Quandela's addition of executives with deep tech scaling experience from established companies like Atos and Eutelsat reflects the growing confidence in photonic quantum computing's commercial potential and the need for proven leadership to navigate international expansion. The recruitment of talent from adjacent high-tech sectors like cybersecurity and aerospace demonstrates how quantum companies are leveraging expertise from industries that have successfully commercialized cutting-edge technologies. This wave of executive appointments across multiple quantum firms suggests the industry is entering a crucial phase where business execution and market strategy are becoming as important as technological breakthroughs.

Quandela has announced a major restructuring of its executive leadership and governance to drive the international scale-up of its photonic quantum computing hardware. Cyril Dujardin, an executive with 25 years of deep tech and cybersecurity experience at Atos and Eutelsat/OneWeb, has been named Chief Operating Officer (COO). Joining him is semiconductor and aerospace operations veteran [...] The post Who’s News: Strategic Leadership Appointments at Quandela, Zapata Quantum, Imperagen, and EigenQ appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Pramatra Space Secures Pre-Seed Capital to Advance Space-Based Quantum Key Distribution Hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This funding round highlights the growing commercial viability of quantum key distribution (QKD) systems, particularly the challenging but promising space-based approach that could enable truly global quantum-secure communications. Pramatra's focus on satellite-based QKD hardware represents a significant technical leap beyond terrestrial fiber-optic systems, as space-based platforms can overcome the distance limitations that have historically constrained quantum cryptography networks. The backing from established space industry veterans like Pixxel's founder suggests increasing confidence that quantum security solutions are moving from laboratory curiosities to practical commercial applications. While terrestrial QKD networks are already operational in some regions, successful deployment of satellite-based systems could democratize access to quantum-secure communications globally, making this an important development to watch as quantum threats to classical encryption continue to evolve.

Pramatra Space Technology Pvt. Ltd., a deep-tech quantum security venture based in Bengaluru, has closed an undisclosed pre-seed financing round led by Seafund Ventures. The investment round included participation from Rebalance, Magnivia Ventures (co-investing with the Peaceful Progress Fund), and angel investors including Awais Ahmed, founder of earth observation company Pixxel Space. This financing supplements [...] The post Pramatra Space Secures Pre-Seed Capital to Advance Space-Based Quantum Key Distribution Hardware appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Stanford quantum computing breakthrough uses twisted light to work without extreme cooling
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Stanford breakthrough represents a potentially game-changing advance by demonstrating quantum entanglement at room temperature using twisted light beams, eliminating the need for the expensive dilution refrigerators that currently keep most quantum computers colder than outer space. The ability to create and manipulate entangled states without extreme cooling could dramatically reduce the cost and complexity of quantum systems, making them far more practical for widespread deployment. While photonic quantum computing has shown promise before, this approach of entangling photons with electrons using orbital angular momentum could open new pathways for hybrid quantum systems that combine the best of optical and electronic quantum technologies. If this technique scales effectively, it could accelerate the timeline for quantum computers to move from specialized research facilities into more accessible commercial applications.

A new room-temperature quantum device uses twisted light to entangle photons and electrons, overcoming one of the biggest hurdles in quantum technology. The breakthrough could pave the way for smaller, cheaper quantum systems with applications ranging from secure communications to future AI and computing platforms.

Read original at Science Daily - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Rigetti Simulates Plasma Wave Dispersion on Superconducting Ankaa-3 Processor Using Specialized Error Mitigation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between Rigetti, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, and the University of Colorado represents a significant step toward practical quantum simulations of complex physical phenomena, specifically demonstrating how quantum computers can model electromagnetic wave behavior in plasma environments. The use of specialized error mitigation techniques on Rigetti's Ankaa-3 superconducting processor is particularly noteworthy, as plasma physics simulations are notoriously computationally intensive and require high fidelity to produce meaningful results. This work could have important implications for fusion energy research and space physics applications, where understanding plasma wave dispersion is crucial for reactor design and satellite communications. The successful demonstration also validates the potential of near-term quantum devices to tackle real-world scientific computing problems when paired with sophisticated error correction methods.

An electromagnetic wave packet propagating (a) in free space, (b) from free space to a sharp jump in plasma density (which mimics the edge of a confined overdense plasma), and (c) through an inhomogeneous plasma with a spatially varying density profile. Rigetti Computing, in collaboration with Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory and the University of Colorado, [...] The post Rigetti Simulates Plasma Wave Dispersion on Superconducting Ankaa-3 Processor Using Specialized Error Mitigation appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Machines Achieves 99.5% Two-Qubit Gate Fidelity Operating Rigetti’s Novera QPU
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between Quantum Machines and Rigetti demonstrates a significant milestone in quantum hardware interoperability, showing that third-party control systems can achieve the same high-fidelity performance as native hardware configurations. The 99.5% two-qubit gate fidelity represents near-threshold performance for fault-tolerant quantum computing, where error correction schemes typically require gate fidelities above 99% to be viable. Perhaps most importantly, the ten-day automated calibration process using Quantum Machines' OPX1000 platform suggests that quantum systems are becoming more plug-and-play, potentially accelerating deployment timelines for quantum computing installations. This cross-vendor compatibility could help standardize the quantum ecosystem and give organizations more flexibility in choosing control hardware that best fits their research or commercial needs.

Quantum Machines (QM) has operated Rigetti Computing’s commercially available Novera superconducting quantum processing unit (QPU) using external control hardware and software, reaching a 99.5% median two-qubit gate fidelity. Configured via the OPX1000 orchestration platform and managed by QUAlibrate automation software, the onsite optimization process achieved full calibration within ten days. The results match the peak [...] The post Quantum Machines Achieves 99.5% Two-Qubit Gate Fidelity Operating Rigetti’s Novera QPU appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: University of Tennessee Launches Knoxville Quantum Accelerator to Anchor State Initiative
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a significant trend where individual states are taking bold initiatives to establish quantum technology hubs, with Tennessee's $43 million investment representing one of the more substantial state-level commitments to date. The Knoxville Quantum Accelerator's focus on hardware validation and workforce training addresses two critical gaps in the quantum ecosystem - the need for practical testing facilities and the shortage of quantum-skilled workers that many companies are facing. What makes this particularly noteworthy is how Tennessee is positioning itself to compete with traditional tech powerhouses by leveraging its advanced manufacturing base and creating localized quantum infrastructure rather than trying to attract existing coastal talent. This state-by-state approach to quantum development could reshape the geographic distribution of quantum innovation across the United States.

The University of Tennessee, Knoxville (UT) has launched the Knoxville Quantum Accelerator, branded as K-Quantum, to coordinate regional quantum technology research, hardware validation, and workforce training. The initiative operates in alignment with the Tennessee Quantum Initiative, a $43 million statewide economic strategy directed by Governor Bill Lee to establish localized infrastructure across advanced manufacturing, life [...] The post University of Tennessee Launches Knoxville Quantum Accelerator to Anchor State Initiative appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement in quantum channel discrimination: sometimes less is more
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article challenges a fundamental assumption in quantum information theory by demonstrating that entanglement isn't always beneficial for quantum channel discrimination tasks. The researchers present concrete examples where maximally entangled states perform no better than random guessing, while separable (non-entangled) states can achieve perfect discrimination between certain quantum channels. This counterintuitive finding has significant implications for quantum sensing and metrology applications, where practitioners typically assume that more entanglement leads to better performance. The work provides a systematic framework for identifying when entanglement helps versus hurts, offering practical guidance for optimizing quantum protocols in sensing and communication systems.

Entanglement is known to be a powerful resource that improves performance in various quantum information and computational tasks. A standard example of such a phenomenon is the possibility of perfectly discriminating all four Pauli operations in a single shot via the superdense coding protocol. While entanglement is often a powerful resource for quantum channel discrimination, this is not necessarily the case. In this work, we identify scenarios in which the maximally entangled state is a bad choice of input state and, more generally, show that excessive entanglement can reduce channel discriminability dramatically. To do so, we present an explicit pair of unitary channels which are perfectly discriminable without entanglement, but for which any strategy with maximally entangled input states is $ε$-close to a blind uniform guessing strategy. To develop a systematic approach, we introduce the concepts of Maximal Entanglement Worst Case (MEWC) and Maximal Entanglement Best Case (MEBC) pairs of channels, and present conditions for a pair of channels to be MEWC or MEBC. With these conditions, we show that the optimal input states for discriminating MEWC pairs of channels are necessarily separable, and provide non-trivial examples of measurement channels for which entanglement necessarily reduces the maximum probability of discrimination.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Experimental demonstration of quantum advantage in communication complexity for Euclidean distance problem
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work represents a significant milestone in quantum communication by demonstrating the first practical quantum advantage for a real-world data analysis task - calculating distances between large datasets like images. The researchers cleverly used coherent light pulses instead of complex entangled qubits to achieve exponential communication savings for inputs as large as 100 million elements, making this one of the most scalable quantum advantage demonstrations to date. What makes this particularly exciting is that it solves an actual problem that appears frequently in machine learning and data science, showing how quantum protocols could eventually reduce bandwidth requirements for distributed computing tasks. The fact that they achieved this advantage with relatively simple optical components and real image data suggests this approach could bridge the gap between quantum theory and practical applications much sooner than previously expected.

When considering the complexity of communication protocols, the aim is to perform a certain task with the minimum amount of communication resources, such as time and transmitted information. The use of quantum states may lead to an exponential advantage in the use of such resources. Here, we are interested in the task of calculating the Euclidean distance between two vectors representing real data sets. It has been previously shown that it is possible to obtain an advantage for this task based on quantum fingerprinting. This protocol is defined in the simultaneous message passing model of communication complexity, where the two parties do not communicate with each other but send data to a third party, and exploits practical fingerprints generated using trains of coherent state pulses instead of highly entangled qubit states that are hard to generate for large input sizes needed to demonstrate an exponential advantage. We perform a proof-of-principle experimental demonstration of the Euclidean distance protocol using amplitude modulation techniques for encoding non-binary data sets and high-performance superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors required to increase the accessible input size. We show a quantum advantage in transmitted information surpassing the best classical protocol for an input size of $10^8$, for diverse types of data sets, including those corresponding to real grayscale images, and with reasonable precision and error bounds. Our results highlight the potential of quantum communication complexity for use in a broad set of applications.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: An efficient Progressive Swapping to the Middle distribution protocol adapted to imperfect quantum memories in quantum networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum networking's most fundamental challenges: how to efficiently distribute entanglement across long distances when quantum memories inevitably degrade over time. The proposed Progressive Swapping to the Middle protocol cleverly combines the best aspects of existing approaches by having entanglement swapping operations work from both ends of a network path toward the center, significantly improving success rates while maintaining reasonable fidelity levels. This work is particularly valuable because it explicitly accounts for the imperfections that plague real quantum hardware, making it more practically relevant than theoretical protocols that assume perfect conditions.
